# Formal Conjectures PR Checklist — `deepmind/910.lean`

Every item must pass before a PR is approved. Reviewers: reject if any box is unchecked
without an explicit justification.

---

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line):
  `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 910`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/910`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (both parts stated clearly).
- [x] Bibliography entry for related paper: `[Ru58] Rudin, M. E., ...`

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos910 ... end Erdos910`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase` (`Erdos910`).
- [x] `open Set Cardinal Topology` appears **before** the namespace block.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- N/A — no custom `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations in this file.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Both problems use the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_910` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib conventions: `snake_case` for terms of Props.
- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line** (e.g., `@[category research solved, AMS 54]`).
- [x] Main theorem has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [x] Variant has a docstring.
- [x] Docstrings reference the source paper `[Ru58]`.

### `answer()` usage

Both parts are yes/no questions ("Does every connected set..."), so **Case 1** applies.

- [x] Main theorem uses `answer(False) ↔ P` format — correct for Case 1.
- [x] Problem is solved (Rudin showed the answer is no), so `answer(False)` is correct.
- [x] The RHS formalizes the question as originally asked (affirmative = the property holds), so `answer(False)` correctly means "no".
- [x] Quantifiers (`∀ (n : ℕ), ...`) go **after** `answer(False)`, not as theorem parameters.
- [x] Part 2 variant likewise uses `answer(False) ↔ P` with quantifiers after `answer(False)`.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[category ...]`.
- [x] Category is `research solved` — correct: the problem is solved by Rudin [Ru58] but not formally verified (proofs use `sorry`).
- [x] Not marked `research open` (would be wrong since it's solved).

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has **exactly one** `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number is listed (`54`).
- [x] AMS `54` is a valid MSC2020 code (General topology).
- N/A — only one AMS number, so ordering/duplicates do not apply.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Both theorems use `by` + newline + indented `sorry` — acceptable multi-line form.
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions (no definitions present).
- [x] No informal proof sketches for the solved problem.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- [x] Variant named `erdos_910.variants.part2` — follows convention.
- [x] Variant lives in the **same file** as the main theorem.
- [x] Variant has its own `@[category research solved, AMS 54]` attributes.
- [x] Variant has a docstring explaining what it formalizes.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used where applicable (`∀`, `∃`, `⊆`, `∧`, `↔`, `→`, `ℕ`, `ℝ`).
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` that disables linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (<100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` — not verified in this review.
- [ ] All five custom linters pass — not verified in this review.

---

**Summary:** The file is well-structured and passes all checklist items that can be verified by inspection. Both parts of the problem are correctly identified as yes/no questions (Case 1), with `answer(False)` matching the known negative resolution by Rudin [Ru58]. The formalization uses `IsEmpty (Homeomorph T S)` for "not homeomorphic" and `Cardinal.continuum < #(connected subsets)` for "more than 2^ℵ₀ connected subsets", both of which are idiomatic Mathlib encodings. Build verification is not included in this review.

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 910
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/910](https://www.erdosproblems.com/910)
    23→
    24→Does every connected set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ contain a connected subset which is not a point
    25→and not homeomorphic to the original set? If $n \geq 2$, does every connected set in
    26→$\mathbb{R}^n$ contain more than $2^{\aleph_0}$ many connected subsets?
    27→
    28→Asked by Erdős in the 1940s, who thought the answer to both questions is yes.
    29→The answer to both is in fact no, as shown by Rudin [Ru58] (conditional on the
    30→continuum hypothesis).
    31→
    32→[Ru58] Rudin, M. E., _A connected subset of the plane_, Fund. Math. 46 (1958), 15-24.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open Set Cardinal Topology
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos910
    38→
    39→/--
    40→Erdős Problem 910, Part 1 (disproved by Rudin [Ru58]):
    41→
    42→Does every connected set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ contain a connected subset which is not a single
    43→point and not homeomorphic to the original set?
    44→-/
    45→@[category research solved, AMS 54]
    46→theorem erdos_910 : answer(False) ↔
    47→    ∀ (n : ℕ), n ≥ 1 →
    48→    ∀ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))),
    49→    IsConnected S →
    50→    ∃ (T : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))),
    51→      T ⊆ S ∧ IsConnected T ∧ T.Nontrivial ∧
    52→      IsEmpty (Homeomorph T S) := by
    53→  sorry
    54→
    55→/--
    56→Erdős Problem 910, Part 2 (disproved by Rudin [Ru58]):
    57→
    58→If $n \geq 2$, does every connected set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ have more than $2^{\aleph_0}$ many
    59→connected subsets?
    60→-/
    61→@[category research solved, AMS 54]
    62→theorem erdos_910.variants.part2 : answer(False) ↔
    63→    ∀ (n : ℕ), n ≥ 2 →
    64→    ∀ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))),
    65→    IsConnected S →
    66→    Cardinal.continuum < #{T : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) // T ⊆ S ∧ IsConnected T} := by
    67→  sorry
    68→
    69→end Erdos910
    70→

file.content:
/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 910

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/910](https://www.erdosproblems.com/910)

Does every connected set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ contain a connected subset which is not a point
and not homeomorphic to the original set? If $n \geq 2$, does every connected set in
$\mathbb{R}^n$ contain more than $2^{\aleph_0}$ many connected subsets?

Asked by Erdős in the 1940s, who thought the answer to both questions is yes.
The answer to both is in fact no, as shown by Rudin [Ru58] (conditional on the
continuum hypothesis).

[Ru58] Rudin, M. E., _A connected subset of the plane_, Fund. Math. 46 (1958), 15-24.
-/

open Set Cardinal Topology

namespace Erdos910

/--
Erdős Problem 910, Part 1 (disproved by Rudin [Ru58]):

Does every connected set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ contain a connected subset which is not a single
point and not homeomorphic to the original set?
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 54]
theorem erdos_910 : answer(False) ↔
    ∀ (n : ℕ), n ≥ 1 →
    ∀ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))),
    IsConnected S →
    ∃ (T : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))),
      T ⊆ S ∧ IsConnected T ∧ T.Nontrivial ∧
      IsEmpty (Homeomorph T S) := by
  sorry

/--
Erdős Problem 910, Part 2 (disproved by Rudin [Ru58]):

If $n \geq 2$, does every connected set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ have more than $2^{\aleph_0}$ many
connected subsets?
-/
@[category research solved, AMS 54]
theorem erdos_910.variants.part2 : answer(False) ↔
    ∀ (n : ℕ), n ≥ 2 →
    ∀ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))),
    IsConnected S →
    Cardinal.continuum < #{T : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) // T ⊆ S ∧ IsConnected T} := by
  sorry

end Erdos910

content.text: The review passed. All checklist items that can be verified by inspection are checked. The only unchecked items are build verification (items 12), which the review explicitly notes were not part of the review scope. No changes needed.
